transcript.transcript: All right. We're recording. Alrighty. Got it. My name is Nancy Inaba, and I am part of the committee working on the Idaho Watercolor Society 50th anniversary. Today's date is October 29th, and I am in Boise with Joyce Green to talk about her history and ongoing work with Idaho Watercolor Society. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho. Commemoration. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, Appreciate and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Joyce, first, let me thank you for your time today. This interview will allow us to reflect on and share your memories and experiences on your history with AWS as we approach our 50th anniversary. I will begin the interview with some general questions to start the conversation, and then we can get into more specific topics from there during the interview. I may adjust the order of the questions to allow a natural flow of topics. So before we jump into the questions, do I do you give permission to record this interview? And can I have you state and spell your first and last name? I do give you permission and I am Joyce Green. J o y c e G r e en. Okay, great. All right, well, um, can you tell me something about yourself? Some thing about where you maybe were born. What brought you to Idaho. Your early history. Okay. Um, I was born in Duluth, Minnesota, and grew up on a farm about 60 miles west of that city. Um, went to the University of Minnesota in Duluth for my undergraduate degree and. Ended up after getting married. I worked in, in the Columbus Museum of Art for several years as curator of education. And from there we moved to Arizona, where, . I taught school and got my master's degree in art education, and from there we we lived in Arizona for about 12 years. I worked in several different school systems. Um, the longest being in Sierra Vista as an elementary art teacher. And then from there we moved up to Idaho and have been here since about 1982. Mm. That's a start. Yeah, that's. Yeah, that's a start. That's more than 40 years. Right. Well, actually, you were here almost from the inception of IWS. Correct. They AWS was started in 1979, um, with Ruth Clark putting an ad in the paper, and I showed up a couple of years later. So about the third president, I'm thinking, or me, possibly the third president, um, Edith Hope had just finished as as president and Julie Rifle was starting. And, uh, the story of how I became a member of the Idaho Watercolor Society is kind of cute, so I want to hear that. Oh, I love it. Okay, well, I arrived in Boise and was looking for how to get involved in the art community here. And so there was an ad in the Yellow Pages for Allied Arts, and it was an address in the Belgravia building on Main Street. So I decided to go there. And of course, Allied Arts was discontinued. By that time, there was no such thing, but there was a. A gallery there with three people in it. Um, Edith Hope, who was the current president of AWS at that time, and Searle Mitchell and Maureen Boyle. So I got to meet all three of them at that point. But also there at the time was, uh, Julie Reifel, who was the incoming president of AWS at that time. She says, well, you ought to join the Watercolor Society. And I said, but I don't do watercolors. So. So it's the first time I became interested to do watercolors. She said, well, you you don't have to do them very many with your art background. You can you can just do one every. You only have to enter something every couple of years. So you can do that. So she finally convinced me I would do that. And then she said, and besides that, we need a vice president. And and so it was just typical of most organizations, I think. So I became vice president and corresponding secretary, and I started doing watercolors, and it felt like coming home. It felt so natural. And I've been doing them ever since. Not the other stuff. Is that right? You gave up your other medium? Yeah. Occasionally. You know, Well, I can do an acrylic if I want to. You know, I'll start it and it kind of gets stuck back in the, in the bin. Mhm. But, uh, watercolor seems like the thing that works best for me, to the point that I have earned signature membership in both the National Watercolor Society and the American Watercolor Society, the two top national ones. So it's taken me a little ways. Um. But I've been a member of the Watercolor Society since then and done all kinds of stuff for them. Right. And didn't you also receive an award from the city of Meridian? I did, can't remember what they called it. The, uh, it's over there on my on my piano. It's it's the It was recognizing you as, uh. As for excellence in the arts, it was it was that for for having achieved excellence in the arts over my long span here. Mhm. Well, you know, I'm looking over your shoulder and I see your family history in that painting. Yes, yes, that is my grandparents. And they were both immigrants from Finland and Sweden. And there they were at their homesteading farm, actually, um. In their 80s. And I thought it was so old then, but it's the age I am now. And they were still sawing wood and planting potatoes and things like that. The other picture is from a trip to China in about 1993, and it was from Xinjiang in the Kashgar. And uh, we went to the Sunday market and those people were there. I left out all the mess around them. Mhm. What's that? Um, can you place that, was it 1973 that you went or 93. I'm sorry. 83. Okay. And so the China was just opening up. Yeah. And we were on a tour that was on the Silk Road. And so we were able to go to Kashgar, which is pretty much interdicted now. Mhm. Well, that's a wonderful, um, expression that you captured on those children with their Mao hats on. Yeah. They were still wearing my Mao uniforms quite a bit in those days. And um, the difference between 1993 China and 2014, China was like night and day. Mhm. That's kind of not here or there. But yeah, I know that you are a traveler of the world. I've been to 83 countries. We counted them up one time, just because we had attended a group where a lady was. A couple were celebrating their 100th country, and we thought, well, how many places have we been? And my husband loved travel, so we traveled a good deal. Mhm. Wow. Yeah. That was probably an interesting time to be in China when it first opened up because they really weren't prepared for tourism. It was quite interesting. The hotel in Kashgar was advertised as having 24 hour hot water. Unfortunately it wasn't the 24 hours we were there, but. But that's what they advertised. Okay, well let's see. So you have been you have been working in the arts your whole life? Pretty much. Yes. Mhm. That's, uh that's actually I mean, you're kind of a pioneer for a woman artist. I never felt that way because I knew so many. Uh huh. You can always change something with watercolor. Well, you know, people think we can't, but the beauty of watercolor is everything leaves a mark. Yeah. And so the whole process is there on the paper for you to see. Uh, you know, on canvas or whatever back thing you're using. But everything leaves a mark. It's sort of like our old bodies. Isn't that the truth? Yeah. Well, um, do you have an outstanding memory or a story that highlights your your involvement with IWS? I know you've told a few. Yeah. Wow. I'd have to think about this for a minute. I have the thing. The thing about IWS, the Watercolor Society, is that opportunity to meet and interact with people. And the beauty of it is all over the state, all over the state that you get to know other artists and, um, over time and interact with them, even especially if you attend the workshops, uh, even someplace else, than, you know, when we get a chance to have them someplace else. There was an experiment for a while with having the show and the annual meeting somewhere else in the state, and, uh, it was to a degree, difficult because our membership is somewhat concentrated , in the southwest region, because there are more people living here. Exactly. But, um, having it, having it be able to travel to the other regions has been really good when we can. And I love to see more. More things happen for the other regions as well, right? I had the opportunity to teach a little workshop up in the North region and of course attend a workshop or teach a workshop in Pocatello, you know, and so, um, getting to meet the other people is it's invaluable. Yes. I kind of have heard this before, that, um, they would send artists to the other regions as kind of an ambassadorship. So you can meet and greet and know each other. And and for a while, the, uh, new president would do like the, kings of ancient England or whatever, and they would go on a progression. And and go to each of the regions and meet the, meet the people there. And, and I think that was a good thing. I don't know if it's been done recently, but I think it's, uh, to a degree it has. Right? You know, in the time of internet and, um, iPhone calls, it seems like it, um, communication is still difficult. Yeah. And it's it's so nice to have the the zoom calls, zoom meetings and things like that, that that means those people can attend. Right. Exactly. And know what's going on. Hopefully. You know, I've been impressed by the quality of the artists that, um, that I had the opportunity to interact with and interview, uh, and their efforts to progress themselves as an artist, but also their responsibility to the organization. Yes. Yes, I think so. Yeah. Outstanding. And I know. I'm getting past the age where I want to do a lot of that, but hopefully I continue to do some things. Right. Well, you certainly are a good ambassador. Well thank you. So can you think of anything else we've missed? Gotta look at the list. Um, no, not I think that it's actually been a delightful interview. Joyce, you're very easy. Because the conversation flowed so easily, I didn't have to ask the question because you just moved right into it. I was thinking about what things that I want to make sure that I include. Mhm. You know, for posterity, since this is sort of a posterity thing. Yeah. And um, hopefully I got them all. Okay. Yeah. I. I'll think of something else that I didn't. You know, I've kind of been looking at the sources of inspiration, friendships, marketing, sort of the business of art, other aspects, workshops, uh, things that keep people motivated and growing as an artist or or what were some of the obstacles to maybe how they would like their career as an artist to be? A lot of watercolor artists are taking it up for the first time, because they finally have time, because their lives are so busy and, and and it's a it's a medium thank the heavens that you can do on your kitchen table. Mhm. Without too much outward effort and, and and um it's ridiculous how much space I, how little space I take to do my work. When it comes right down to it, my studio is more taken up with storage than it is with actually working because, um, we have this joke that it's against the law to throw away any art supply. Mhm. So that sort of silts up and then you have works that haven't sold, but are there and works that are in progress. And so there's, there's an awful lot of things that go along with it. But the actual painting doesn't take up a lot of space. So people can do it in the, you know, like I said, on their kitchen table or on the kitchen counter. Right. How cool is that? That's a it's a wonderful thing about watercolor. We should we should celebrate that. Exactly. It's very it doesn't stink up the house. That's my biggest point. And it doesn't ruin your clothes. Right. Well, there are some that have been ruined by miskit. The masking fluid. Because if you if you don't see it and it's in there, it's hard to get it out later. I just noticed I had some, uh, acrylic on my pants. These black pants will be forever changed. Artist who says that she had two kinds of clothes in her closet. Those that have paint on them and those that don't have paint on them yet. Yes, exactly. Exactly. Well, um, I'd like to thank you for your time. Joyce. It's been delightful to talk to you. Well, hopefully this is what we needed. And I'm hopeful to actually talk with some of the beginning artists of the the first presidents from back when and get them on to recording, too. So doing it with you is is one way to learn how to do it myself. And I appreciate it. You're wonderful. Tomorrow. And yeah, it's always, like I said, a miracle when it works. But it's really not that hard. Yeah, I'm. I'm discovering that even though even though I didn't, I had made a mess of it. You were able to find a way to make it work. So kudos to you. And and and living through a few mistakes means that I can handle it. Oh, gosh. I've learned to forgive myself many times over because mistakes come with the territory. They do. And that's. And you know, if you've done if you've done it the wrong way once and you didn't in this case, but if you have done it something the wrong way once. Um, sometimes that's the the best educator. Yes. And the same same goes with painting. You know, the one thing about beginner painters that I'm going to say here is too often they want to jump right into the medium and get going painting. And I understand the impetus, but I started with this huge background in a combination of learning, drawing and design. And those are the the, the basics for being an artist. You can't start jump right into painting. You'll end up with mistakes in drawing and you'll end up with mistakes in composition. And if you've had some of the the lessons in those two things, it's so much easier to do any of the, any of the mediums. Right? There's so many principles and basic skills that come into a good painting. And there's and one needs to study all those little parts so you can actually start to write a whole sentence. I hate to see a beautifully done watercolor or painting ruined because the drawing was wrong. Yeah, I know. I know that from a personal experience. You know, and and so how did how to make your mistakes in drawing first before you get into painting it and fixing them? It just it just saves you a lot of grief. Um, maybe it's part of the name, the Watercolor Society, that you just skip the drawing and the planning. Well, if you. And if you're starting when you retire as an as a painter, you want to get in and paint, I understand that. The other thing I hate to see too often is copying. Copying in order.... For learning is one thing, but then I think it needs to be torn up and thrown away. Right. Yeah. Or giving given, uh, attribution, you know. Yeah. Like trying to reproduce a, uh, a Winslow Homer watercolor would be a real learning experience. Right, exactly. I, I think it helps sometimes you don't want to get stuck in any one way, but it helps to see things. Yeah. It helps also to to be able. I mean, we we now have YouTube videos and all kinds of things where you can watch somebody else do something. Um. And see what how they went about it. That's not a Bible. Yeah, that's not the way to do it. It's a way to do it. Mhm. And and you know, I've always been a little bit um. Leery of artists who teach you this is the way to do something. Because the thing about artists is there's more than one way to do this thing, and you have to find your way. Um, yes. It is definitely a personal journey, as well as learning from. As much as the the. Yeah, yeah, the whole gamut of of of possibilities. Right. And then that sheet of paper is in front of you. And that's a whole gamut of possibilities. I know it's the challenge of the white paper. Yes. Okay, well, okay. That's enough stuff, isn't it? No. You're right. An hour. and a minute. Thank you so much. I appreciate your time. We did good. Yeah, we did good. Thank you. Oh. Thank you. I'll see you tomorrow at Pam. Tomorrow. Okay. Alright. Thanks a lot. All right. Bye bye. Bye bye.

How Joyce got involved with IWS

lines.line: # Introduction and Permissions

> Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and experiences of Joyce Green with the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of a broader oral history project. She outlines the structure of the interview, seeks Joyce's permission to record, and asks for her name and its spelling. This sets the stage for a formal and respectful conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Permissions
metadata.synopsis: Nancy Inaba introduces herself and the purpose of the interview, which is to document the history and experiences of Joyce Green with the Idaho Watercolor Society as part of a broader oral history project. She outlines the structure of the interview, seeks Joyce's permission to record, and asks for her name and its spelling. This sets the stage for a formal and respectful conversation.

metadata.title: Volunteering
23:22 Planning, drawing spontaneity.
45:19 Push towards excellance and inclusiveness- Rotunda

lines.line: # Joyce Green's Early Life and Artistic Background

> Joyce Green shares her origins in Minnesota, her upbringing on a farm, and her educational journey at the University of Minnesota in Duluth. She describes her early career as a curator of education at the Columbus Museum of Art, her move to Arizona, and her work as an art teacher while earning a master's degree in art education. Her eventual relocation to Idaho in 1982 marks the beginning of her long-term connection to the state and its art community.

metadata.title: Joyce Green's Early Life and Artistic Background
metadata.synopsis: Joyce Green shares her origins in Minnesota, her upbringing on a farm, and her educational journey at the University of Minnesota in Duluth. She describes her early career as a curator of education at the Columbus Museum of Art, her move to Arizona, and her work as an art teacher while earning a master's degree in art education. Her eventual relocation to Idaho in 1982 marks the beginning of her long-term connection to the state and its art community.

lines.line: # Joining the Idaho Watercolor Society

> Joyce recounts how she became involved with the Idaho Watercolor Society (IWS) shortly after its founding. She describes her initial search for an art community in Boise, her serendipitous meeting with key IWS members, and her recruitment into the organization despite not being a watercolorist at the time. Her early involvement quickly led to leadership roles, reflecting the welcoming and sometimes urgent needs of volunteer organizations.

metadata.title: Joining the Idaho Watercolor Society
metadata.synopsis: Joyce recounts how she became involved with the Idaho Watercolor Society (IWS) shortly after its founding. She describes her initial search for an art community in Boise, her serendipitous meeting with key IWS members, and her recruitment into the organization despite not being a watercolorist at the time. Her early involvement quickly led to leadership roles, reflecting the welcoming and sometimes urgent needs of volunteer organizations.

lines.line: # Transition to Watercolor and Artistic Achievements

> Joyce explains her transition from other art mediums to watercolor, describing how it felt natural and fulfilling. She discusses her dedication to watercolor, her recognition as a signature member of both the National and American Watercolor Societies, and her receipt of an award for excellence in the arts from the city of Meridian. This chapter highlights her professional growth and the accolades she has earned through her commitment to watercolor.

metadata.title: Transition to Watercolor and Artistic Achievements
metadata.synopsis: Joyce explains her transition from other art mediums to watercolor, describing how it felt natural and fulfilling. She discusses her dedication to watercolor, her recognition as a signature member of both the National and American Watercolor Societies, and her receipt of an award for excellence in the arts from the city of Meridian. This chapter highlights her professional growth and the accolades she has earned through her commitment to watercolor.

lines.line: # Family, Travel, and Artistic Inspiration

> Joyce shares stories about her family heritage, particularly her grandparents who were immigrants and homesteaders, and how their legacy is reflected in her artwork. She also discusses her extensive travels, including a memorable trip to China in the early 1990s, and how these experiences have provided rich material and inspiration for her paintings. Her global perspective and personal history deeply inform her artistic vision.

metadata.title: Family, Travel, and Artistic Inspiration
metadata.synopsis: Joyce shares stories about her family heritage, particularly her grandparents who were immigrants and homesteaders, and how their legacy is reflected in her artwork. She also discusses her extensive travels, including a memorable trip to China in the early 1990s, and how these experiences have provided rich material and inspiration for her paintings. Her global perspective and personal history deeply inform her artistic vision.

lines.line: # Lifelong Commitment to Art and Gender in the Arts

> Joyce reflects on her lifelong passion for art, recalling her childhood ambition to become an artist. She discusses the experience of being a woman in the art world, noting that while many women were artists, they often lacked recognition compared to men. She also touches on her disinterest in the business side of art, preferring to focus on the creative process, and the importance of having support systems for artists.

metadata.title: Lifelong Commitment to Art and Gender in the Arts
metadata.synopsis: Joyce reflects on her lifelong passion for art, recalling her childhood ambition to become an artist. She discusses the experience of being a woman in the art world, noting that while many women were artists, they often lacked recognition compared to men. She also touches on her disinterest in the business side of art, preferring to focus on the creative process, and the importance of having support systems for artists.

lines.line: # Early Roles and Volunteerism in IWS

> Joyce describes her initial roles within the Idaho Watercolor Society, including vice president and corresponding secretary. She emphasizes the value of small gestures like thank-you notes and the importance of volunteer work in sustaining the organization. She also discusses her involvement in organizing annual exhibitions and the challenges that come with such responsibilities, including dealing with logistical issues and member expectations.

metadata.title: Early Roles and Volunteerism in IWS
metadata.synopsis: Joyce describes her initial roles within the Idaho Watercolor Society, including vice president and corresponding secretary. She emphasizes the value of small gestures like thank-you notes and the importance of volunteer work in sustaining the organization. She also discusses her involvement in organizing annual exhibitions and the challenges that come with such responsibilities, including dealing with logistical issues and member expectations.

lines.line: # Leadership and Organizational Challenges

> Joyce recounts her eventual presidency of IWS, the difficulties in recruiting volunteers, and the common excuses people give for not participating. She humorously compares the situation to the 'farmer in the dell' and acknowledges the frustrations and rewards of leading a volunteer-driven organization. She expresses appreciation for the current volunteers and her own decision to step back from major roles while still contributing where she can.

metadata.title: Leadership and Organizational Challenges
metadata.synopsis: Joyce recounts her eventual presidency of IWS, the difficulties in recruiting volunteers, and the common excuses people give for not participating. She humorously compares the situation to the 'farmer in the dell' and acknowledges the frustrations and rewards of leading a volunteer-driven organization. She expresses appreciation for the current volunteers and her own decision to step back from major roles while still contributing where she can.

lines.line: # The Value of Community and Artistic Growth in IWS

> The discussion centers on the benefits of volunteering and community involvement within IWS. Joyce highlights the importance of social interaction, critique sessions, and the supportive environment that fosters artistic growth. She notes that the society's open membership policy allows for a diverse range of artists to develop their skills and be inspired by one another, contributing to both personal and collective advancement.

metadata.title: The Value of Community and Artistic Growth in IWS
metadata.synopsis: The discussion centers on the benefits of volunteering and community involvement within IWS. Joyce highlights the importance of social interaction, critique sessions, and the supportive environment that fosters artistic growth. She notes that the society's open membership policy allows for a diverse range of artists to develop their skills and be inspired by one another, contributing to both personal and collective advancement.

lines.line: # Artistic Influences, Style, and Process

> Joyce discusses how living in Idaho has influenced her artistic subjects and color palette, shifting from warm tones in Arizona to cooler ones in Idaho. She describes her attraction to painting movement—people, animals, and water—and her background in abstract expressionism, which informs her compositional choices even as she works in a more realistic style. She reflects on the balance between planning and spontaneity in her creative process.

metadata.title: Artistic Influences, Style, and Process
metadata.synopsis: Joyce discusses how living in Idaho has influenced her artistic subjects and color palette, shifting from warm tones in Arizona to cooler ones in Idaho. She describes her attraction to painting movement—people, animals, and water—and her background in abstract expressionism, which informs her compositional choices even as she works in a more realistic style. She reflects on the balance between planning and spontaneity in her creative process.

lines.line: # Artistic Structure, Binge Painting, and Teaching

> Joyce describes herself as a 'binge artist,' working intensely in bursts of creativity and then pausing when life intervenes. She discusses the importance of building momentum in her work, the challenges of maintaining discipline, and her experiences teaching art at various levels. She shares her current preference for teaching workshops over ongoing classes, valuing the opportunity to inspire others without the demands of continuous instruction.

metadata.title: Artistic Structure, Binge Painting, and Teaching
metadata.synopsis: Joyce describes herself as a 'binge artist,' working intensely in bursts of creativity and then pausing when life intervenes. She discusses the importance of building momentum in her work, the challenges of maintaining discipline, and her experiences teaching art at various levels. She shares her current preference for teaching workshops over ongoing classes, valuing the opportunity to inspire others without the demands of continuous instruction.

lines.line: # Mentorship, Workshops, and Artistic Inspiration

> Joyce reflects on the artists who have inspired her, including Winslow Homer, and the value of taking workshops to overcome creative impasses. She emphasizes the importance of learning from both renowned artists and peers, and her willingness to share her own techniques openly. Joyce believes that workshops are most beneficial when they provide new insights at critical moments in an artist's development, and she values the inspiration gained from travel and community.

metadata.title: Mentorship, Workshops, and Artistic Inspiration
metadata.synopsis: Joyce reflects on the artists who have inspired her, including Winslow Homer, and the value of taking workshops to overcome creative impasses. She emphasizes the importance of learning from both renowned artists and peers, and her willingness to share her own techniques openly. Joyce believes that workshops are most beneficial when they provide new insights at critical moments in an artist's development, and she values the inspiration gained from travel and community.

lines.line: # Community Engagement and Notable Incidents in IWS

> Joyce recounts a significant censorship incident involving an IWS exhibition at Boise Cascade, which led to changes in the society's exhibition policies. She discusses the balance between artistic freedom and community standards, the role of artists in challenging norms, and the evolution of the society's approach to public exhibitions. This chapter highlights the complexities of community engagement and the importance of inclusivity and dialogue in the arts.

metadata.title: Community Engagement and Notable Incidents in IWS
metadata.synopsis: Joyce recounts a significant censorship incident involving an IWS exhibition at Boise Cascade, which led to changes in the society's exhibition policies. She discusses the balance between artistic freedom and community standards, the role of artists in challenging norms, and the evolution of the society's approach to public exhibitions. This chapter highlights the complexities of community engagement and the importance of inclusivity and dialogue in the arts.

lines.line: # Inclusivity, Excellence, and the Future of IWS

> The conversation explores the ongoing challenge of balancing inclusivity with the pursuit of artistic excellence within IWS. Joyce discusses the need for more abstract and large-scale works, the impact of exhibition constraints, and the importance of regional and inter-society collaboration. She reflects on the society's growth, the value of open shows like the rotunda exhibition, and the need to continually broaden opportunities for members.

metadata.title: Inclusivity, Excellence, and the Future of IWS
metadata.synopsis: The conversation explores the ongoing challenge of balancing inclusivity with the pursuit of artistic excellence within IWS. Joyce discusses the need for more abstract and large-scale works, the impact of exhibition constraints, and the importance of regional and inter-society collaboration. She reflects on the society's growth, the value of open shows like the rotunda exhibition, and the need to continually broaden opportunities for members.

lines.line: # Concerns about the Future and Youth Engagement

> Joyce expresses concern about the declining interest in watercolor among young people, noting the discontinuation of watercolor instruction at Boise State University. She challenges the perception of watercolor as merely a sketching medium and advocates for its recognition as a serious and permanent art form. Joyce emphasizes the importance of completed works and the unique qualities of watercolor, including its transparency and the permanence of every mark.

metadata.title: Concerns about the Future and Youth Engagement
metadata.synopsis: Joyce expresses concern about the declining interest in watercolor among young people, noting the discontinuation of watercolor instruction at Boise State University. She challenges the perception of watercolor as merely a sketching medium and advocates for its recognition as a serious and permanent art form. Joyce emphasizes the importance of completed works and the unique qualities of watercolor, including its transparency and the permanence of every mark.

lines.line: # Statewide Connections and Regional Outreach

> Joyce highlights the value of connecting with artists across Idaho through IWS, including the benefits of regional workshops, traveling exhibitions, and leadership outreach. She recalls efforts to bring the annual meeting to different parts of the state and the importance of face-to-face interactions, even as technology like Zoom has improved communication. These statewide connections enrich the society and foster a sense of unity among Idaho artists.

metadata.title: Statewide Connections and Regional Outreach
metadata.synopsis: Joyce highlights the value of connecting with artists across Idaho through IWS, including the benefits of regional workshops, traveling exhibitions, and leadership outreach. She recalls efforts to bring the annual meeting to different parts of the state and the importance of face-to-face interactions, even as technology like Zoom has improved communication. These statewide connections enrich the society and foster a sense of unity among Idaho artists.

lines.line: # Accessibility of Watercolor and Practical Aspects

> Joyce discusses the accessibility and practicality of watercolor as a medium, noting that many artists take it up later in life due to its convenience and minimal space requirements. She humorously mentions the accumulation of art supplies and the realities of storage, as well as the non-toxic and non-messy nature of watercolor compared to other mediums. This accessibility makes watercolor appealing to a wide range of people.

metadata.title: Accessibility of Watercolor and Practical Aspects
metadata.synopsis: Joyce discusses the accessibility and practicality of watercolor as a medium, noting that many artists take it up later in life due to its convenience and minimal space requirements. She humorously mentions the accumulation of art supplies and the realities of storage, as well as the non-toxic and non-messy nature of watercolor compared to other mediums. This accessibility makes watercolor appealing to a wide range of people.

lines.line: # Artistic Fundamentals, Copying, and Learning

> Joyce emphasizes the importance of foundational skills such as drawing and design before diving into painting. She cautions against skipping these basics, as mistakes in drawing and composition can undermine otherwise well-executed paintings. Joyce also discusses the role of copying as a learning tool, advocating for proper attribution and the value of learning from various sources, including YouTube and workshops, while ultimately finding one's own artistic voice.

metadata.title: Artistic Fundamentals, Copying, and Learning

She cautions against skipping these basics, as mistakes in drawing and composition can undermine otherwise well-executed paintings. Joyce also discusses the role of copying as a learning tool, advocating for proper attribution and the value of learning from various sources, including YouTube and workshops, while ultimately finding one's own artistic voice.

lines.line: # Closing Remarks and Reflections

> The interview concludes with reflections on the creative process, the challenge of starting new work, and the importance of learning from mistakes. Joyce and Nancy express gratitude for the conversation, acknowledge the value of perseverance and self-forgiveness, and reiterate the personal and evolving nature of the artistic journey. The session ends on a note of camaraderie and mutual appreciation.

> Joyce emphasizes the importance of foundational skills such as drawing and design before diving into painting. She cautions against skipping these basics, as mistakes in drawing and composition can undermine otherwise well-executed paintings. Joyce also discusses the role of copying as a learning tool, advocating for proper attribution and the value of learning from various sources, including YouTube and workshops, while ultimately finding one's own artistic voice.
# Closing Remarks and Reflections

> The interview concludes with reflections on the creative process, the challenge of starting new work, and the importance of learning from mistakes. Joyce and Nancy express gratitude for the conversation, acknowledge the value of perseverance and self-forgiveness, and reiterate the personal and evolving nature of the artistic journey. The session ends on a note of camaraderie and mutual appreciation.
